docs.title: CfD Payment Analysis
docs.text: Welcome to the CfD Payment Analysis documentation — a comprehensive analysis of UK Contracts for Difference (CfD) renewable energy subsidy payments.

docs.title: What are CfDs?
docs.text:
Contracts for Difference (CfDs) are the UK government's main mechanism for supporting low-carbon electricity generation. They work by:

  1. Guaranteeing a "strike price" — a fixed price per MWh of electricity generated
  2. Topping up the market price — when wholesale prices are low, generators receive payments to bring their revenue up to the strike price
  3. Clawing back excess — when wholesale prices are high, generators pay money back

The result is price certainty for renewable generators and cost predictability for consumers.

docs.title: About This Analysis
docs.text:
This project analyzes real-world CfD performance data from 2015 onwards, covering:

  • 💷 Subsidy costs — how much consumers have paid and what we got for it
  • ⚡ Generation patterns — capacity factors, seasonality, and intermittency
  • 📊 Cost-effectiveness — bang for buck, CO₂ abatement costs
  • 📉 Market impacts — price cannibalisation and capture ratios

All charts feature:

  • 🌙 Dark theme matching Energy Dashboard UK aesthetic
  • 📱 Interactive visualizations built with Plotly
  • 🐦 Twitter-ready exports (1200×675 PNG)
  • ℹ️ Info icons linking to detailed methodology

docs.title: Subsidy Economics
docs.text:
Analyze the costs and value of CfD subsidies:

  • Bang for Buck — Subsidy received vs CO₂ avoided per project
  • CfD vs Gas Cost — Total cost comparison with gas counterfactual
  • Scissors Chart — Strike prices vs market prices over time
  • Lorenz Curve — Concentration of subsidies across projects
  • Remaining Obligations — Future payment commitments

docs.title: Capacity Factor
docs.text:
Understand renewable generation performance:

  • Monthly Trends — Capacity factor by technology over time
  • Seasonal Patterns — How weather affects generation

docs.title: Intermittency
docs.text:
Explore the challenges of variable generation:

  • Generation Heatmap — Daily capacity factors visualized as wind stripes
  • Load Duration — How often different output levels occur
  • Rolling Minimum — Dunkelflaute events and minimum output windows

docs.title: Cannibalisation
docs.text:
Examine how renewables affect their own economics:

  • Capture Ratio — Wind selling into cheaper hours as capacity grows
  • Price vs Wind — Market price correlation with wind output

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
All analysis uses publicly available data:

  • LCCC — Actual CfD Generation and avoided GHG emissions
  • Elexon/BMRS — Balancing Mechanism Reporting Service data
  • National Grid ESO — Carbon Intensity API
  • ONS — System Average Price of gas

See Data Sources for detailed information.

docs.title: Dark Theme
docs.text:
All charts use a dark navy theme (#1a1d29) matching the Energy Dashboard UK aesthetic, with:

  • Left-aligned bold titles
  • Cyan accent colors (#00d9ff)
  • High contrast for readability
  • Info icons (ⓘ) for methodology

docs.title: Interactive Charts
docs.text:
• Hover for detailed values
  • Click legend items to show/hide traces
  • Zoom and pan on charts
  • Download as static images

docs.title: Twitter Integration
docs.text: Every chart exports a Twitter-optimized PNG (1200×675) perfect for sharing insights on social media.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Our analysis follows these principles:

  1. Transparency — All calculations documented and reproducible
  2. Primary sources — Direct from LCCC, Elexon, National Grid
  3. Daily granularity — Captures price spikes and generation patterns
  4. Lifetime perspective — Cumulative totals from project start to present
  5. Like-for-like comparisons — Gas counterfactuals use actual generation profiles

See Methodology for detailed explanations.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Browse the charts by category using the navigation menu, or:

  1. Start with Bang for Buck — A high-level view of value for money
  2. Explore CfD vs Gas — See if CfDs saved or cost money
  3. Check Cannibalisation — Understand the self-defeating economics

docs.title: About
docs.text:
This analysis is an independent examination of publicly available CfD data. It aims to:

  • 📊 Inform public debate with transparent, data-driven analysis
  • 🔍 Highlight trade-offs between carbon reduction and cost
  • 📈 Track real-world performance against policy goals
  • 🌍 Contribute to energy policy discussions

All code and analysis is available on GitHub.

Questions or feedback? Open an issue or contribute improvements.

docs.title: Charts Overview
docs.text: This section contains 14 interactive visualizations analyzing UK CfD renewable energy subsidies, generation patterns, and market impacts. All charts feature a dark theme, interactive controls, and Twitter-ready exports.

docs.text:

docs.text:

docs.text:

docs.text:
Each chart page includes:

docs.text:
All chart outputs are available in the output/ directory:

  • HTML files: output/*.html (interactive)
  • Twitter PNGs: output/*_twitter.png (1200×675)

docs.title: 🛠️ Technical Details
docs.text:
Charts are built using:

  • Python 3.13 + Plotly 6.7.0
  • Data sources: LCCC, Elexon, National Grid ESO, ONS
  • Update frequency: Data refreshed when new monthly reports published
  • ChartBuilder: Custom framework for consistent styling

See Methodology for complete technical documentation.

Ready to explore? Choose a category above or browse individual charts using the navigation menu.

docs.title: Bang for Buck — Subsidy vs CO₂ Avoided
Overview
docs.text: The Bang for Buck chart visualizes the cost-effectiveness of individual CfD projects by plotting total subsidy received against total CO₂ emissions avoided. Each dot represents a CfD unit, with the size indicating total generation (GWh) and color showing the allocation round.

docs.title: Key Insights
docs.text:
• Top-left = Good value: Projects that avoided significant carbon for relatively low subsidy
  • Bottom-right = Poor value: Projects that received large subsidies but avoided less carbon
  • The UK ETS carbon price (£50/tCO₂) divides the chart into two zones:
    • 🟢 Green zone (above line): Cost-effective — avoided carbon for less than the market carbon price
    • 🔴 Red zone (below line): Not cost-effective — paid more per tonne than carbon costs on the open market

docs.title: Axes
docs.text:
• X-axis: Total subsidy received (£ millions) — logarithmic scale
  • Y-axis: Total CO₂ avoided (kt) — logarithmic scale

docs.title: Visual Elements
docs.text:
• Dot size: Total generation (GWh) — larger dots = more electricity generated
  • Dot color: Allocation round (Investment Contract, AR1, AR2, AR4, AR5, AR6)
  • Log scales: Spread out clustered small projects for better visibility

docs.title: Reference Line
docs.text: The dashed red line represents the UK ETS carbon price of £50/tCO₂. This is the cost of carbon on the open market: - Projects above this line avoided carbon more cheaply than buying carbon credits - Projects below this line cost more per tonne than the market carbon price

docs.title: Data Source
docs.text: LCCC "Actual CfD Generation and avoided GHG emissions" dataset

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text:
Subsidy (X-axis): 

Subsidy = Σ(Strike Price - Market Price) × Generation
This is the actual levy payment — the premium consumers paid above the market price.

CO₂ Avoided (Y-axis): 

CO₂ Avoided = Σ(Avoided GHG tonnes CO₂e)
Total greenhouse gas emissions avoided by the project over its lifetime.

Cost per tonne: 

£/tCO₂ = Subsidy (£) / CO₂ Avoided (tonnes)

docs.title: Filters Applied
docs.text:
• Positive subsidies only: Units with negative total payments (net clawback) excluded — they received no net subsidy so £/tCO₂ is meaningless
  • Minimum generation: Units with < 100 GWh total generation excluded to remove noise from newly commissioned projects

docs.title: Good Value Projects
docs.text: Projects in the top-left quadrant: - Low subsidy relative to CO₂ avoided - High carbon abatement per £ spent - Better value than the carbon market price

docs.title: Poor Value Projects
docs.text: Projects in the bottom-right quadrant: - High subsidy relative to CO₂ avoided - Low carbon abatement per £ spent - Worse value than buying carbon credits

docs.title: What Affects Position?
docs.text:
1. Technology efficiency: More efficient technologies generate more per MW, avoiding more carbon
  2. Capacity factor: Wind/solar intermittency affects total generation over lifetime
  3. Strike price: Higher strike prices increase subsidy costs
  4. Market prices during operation: Lower market prices = higher subsidy payments
  5. Project timing: Earlier projects may have different cost structures

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
Observations

  • Investment Contracts (early projects) tend to have higher subsidies relative to CO₂ avoided
  • Later allocation rounds generally show improved cost-effectiveness
  • Offshore wind projects dominate the high-generation, high-CO₂-avoided quadrant
  • Some large projects received substantial subsidies but the CO₂ benefit varied significantly

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Caveats

  • Does not account for the full system value of renewable generation (grid stability, energy security, etc.)
  • UK ETS price (£50/tCO₂) is a snapshot — carbon prices fluctuate
  • Does not include non-carbon benefits (air quality, health impacts)
  • Lifetime perspective: Early projects may look expensive but enabled technology learning curves
  • Gas counterfactual assumes all displaced generation would be gas — reality is more complex

docs.title: Related Charts
docs.text:

  • Subsidy per tCO₂ — Time series view of cost per tonne avoided
